content.text: A contract is a destination. A mission is every room between you and it.

A mission is a node map: entry at the bottom, objective at the top, 2–4 branching routes, 6–14 nodes for a standard contract. Players see the map skeleton plus whatever intel state each node has earned (see Intel).
Node types
One node = one prep card
A node card is one prep unit: type, occupants, profiles, terrain, exits, one secret. A GM preps ten cards, not a dungeon.
Occupants go on the card as profile cards (see Enemy Framework). When a node reaches Scouted, the players get the card's count, taxonomy, and armor profile — the states live on Intel.
Geometry is pacing
Map width = player agency. More routes, more meaningful choice.
Map height = session length. More nodes per route, longer run.
Route-not-taken content is never wasted — unvisited nodes recycle into the next map.
Pacing is geometry, and the geometry is fully yours.
Rest nodes — where the shell clock meets the map
A ⌂ node is field repair with its permanent-consequence rules (see Armor), reloads (see Consumables & Ammo), and a breather for Saturation (see Charge & Saturation).

Route choice is the armor economy. Push the short route with a cracking shell, or detour to the safe room and patch — that is the decision the map exists to pose. Pipeline → wear → intel → route, one loop.

content.label: DESIGN GAP
content.text: No traversal rule is printed — what moving node-to-node costs in time or resources. “Time ticks” appear only as an in-mission scouting cost (see Intel); no mission clock exists in the Phase 7 docs.
The booklet format
A mission booklet is self-contained:
The map.
One node card per node.
Three corp-flavored briefing variants — same mission, three different lies (see Corp Briefings).
A complication table.
Booklets are Guide work product — routes and maps are what a Guide sells (see Guide).
Route data is provided as-is. The Crowned Table certifies that its maps were accurate at the time of survey. The time of survey is available upon request.
